Homework Assignment 1 The Art of Analyzing Big Data :The Data Scientist's Toolbox By Dr. Michael Fire  General Guidelines • LLM Usage: You are encouraged to use LLMs (ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot, etc.) to help you write code. • Understanding Requirement: For every task, you must submit a written explanation (in your own words, without LLM assistance) that covers: o What you built and how it works o Which technologies/libraries you chose and why o What challenges you encountered and how you solved them o It is critical that you understand what was done :not just let the LLM do 99% of the work. You may be asked to explain your solution in person. • Submission format: For each task, submit one of the following: § A link to a deployed web app (Streamlit Cloud / HuggingFace Spaces / GitHub Pages / Google Colab) :preferred § A Jupyter/Colab notebook with embedded interactive widgets § A screen recording (3–5 min, with narration explaining your ﬁndings) uploaded to YouTube or Google Drive § A GitHub repository with a README and screenshots • Along with your code, submit a short written report (PDF or markdown, 1–2 pages per task) with your personal explanation as described above, plus any interesting ﬁndings.     
Task 1: Baby Names Explorer (25pt) Dataset: US Baby Names (use NationalNames.csv, and optionally StateNames.csv) Backend requirement: SQLite via the sqlite3 Python package. Task 1.1: Data Loading & Schema Design (5pt) • Load the CSV into a SQLite database with appropriate table(s) and columns (Id, Name, Year, Gender, Count, and State if using state data). • Create at least 2 indexes and justify your choice in a markdown cell :explain which queries they speed up and why. Task 1.2: Interactive Name Explorer App (15pt) Build an interactive application using a framework of your choice (Streamlit, Gradio, Panel, Dash, ipywidgets, or any other). The app must include all of the following features: A. Name Popularity Over Time • The user types one or more names (comma-separated) and sees a line chart of the name's popularity across years. • Include a toggle to switch between raw count and percentage of all births that year (relative popularity). B. Custom SQL Query Panel • A text box where the user can type and run any SELECT query against the database. • Results are displayed as a table and, where applicable, as a chart (if the result contains a numeric column and a time/categorical column, o`er a bar or line chart). • Include at least 3 pre-built example queries as clickable buttons or a dropdown that auto-ﬁll the query box (e.g., "Top 10 names in 2010" , "Gender-neutral names" , "Names that disappeared after 1950"). • Safety: Only allow SELECT statements. Show a clear, friendly error message for any non-SELECT query.     
C. One Additional Visualization of Your Choice Pick at least one, or invent your own: • Animated "bar chart race" of top names per decade • A gender-neutral name ﬁnder (names used for both M and F) • "Your name's peak decade" feature • Name diversity over time (how many unique names per year) • Any other creative idea  Task 1.3: Pattern Discovery (10pt) Use your app to ﬁnd and report 3 interesting patterns in the data. For each pattern: • State the ﬁnding clearly • Show the query or visualization that reveals it • Write a one-paragraph interpretation (what might explain this pattern?) Examples of what counts: cultural shifts in naming trends, name popularity spikes after celebrity events, gender crossover names, increasing name diversity over time, regional di`erences (if using state data).  Task 2: Oscar Actor Explorer (25pt) Dataset: The Oscar Award Requirement: Use an ORM :PonyORM, SQLAlchemy, or Peewee (your choice). No raw SQL allowed for this task. Task 2.1 :Data Modeling with ORM (5pt) • Load the dataset into SQLite using your chosen ORM. • Deﬁne proper entity/model classes with appropriate types, relationships, and constraints. • In a markdown cell: brieﬂy explain your schema design and why you chose this ORM over others.   
Task 2.2: Actor Proﬁle App (10pt) Build an interactive app or widget. The user enters an actor or director name and gets a rich proﬁle card that combines: From the dataset: • Number of nominations and wins • Categories nominated in • Years active at the Oscars • List of nominated and winning ﬁlms From Wikipedia (fetched live via the Wikipedia API or the wikipedia Python package): • Short biography summary • Birth date and photo (if available) Computed insights: • Win rate (wins / nominations) • Comparison to the average nominee in their category • Years between ﬁrst nomination and ﬁrst win (if applicable) Handle edge cases gracefully: actor not found in dataset, ambiguous Wikipedia matches, actors with no wins, etc. Task 2.3 :Interesting Finds (10pt) Use your app and/or ORM queries to ﬁnd and report 3 interesting discoveries. For each: state the ﬁnding, show how you found it, and explain why it's interesting. Examples of what might count: • Actors with the most nominations but zero wins  • The longest gap between ﬁrst nomination and ﬁrst win • Directors who were also nominated as actors (or vice versa) • Categories with the most unique winners (hardest to predict) • Actors or ﬁlms with nominations across the most di`erent categories 
Bonus (5pt): Add a "Did You Know?" feature that auto-generates a fun fact when the user looks up any actor (e.g., "Meryl Streep has more nominations than 85% of all Oscar-nominated actors").  Task 3: Pokémon Battle Arena (25pt) Dataset: Pokémon Dataset (or any comprehensive Pokémon stats dataset) Requirement: SQLite via sqlite3 or any ORM. All Pokémon stats must be read from the database :no hardcoded values. Task 3.1 :Data Loading & Schema (5pt) • Load the Pokémon dataset into SQLite (columns like: Name, Type1, Type2, HP , Attack, Defense, Sp.Atk, Sp.Def, Speed, Generation, Legendary). • You may add additional tables if needed (e.g., a type-e`ectiveness table, a battle history log). • In a markdown cell: explain your schema choices. Task 3.2 :Battle Game (10pt) Build a playable Pokémon-style battle game (text-based, widget, or full app :your choice) with: • Team selection: Each player picks 1–3 Pokémon from the database. • Battle mechanics driven by data: Damage, turn order, and e`ectiveness must be calculated from database columns (e.g., Speed determines who goes ﬁrst, Attack vs. Defense determines damage). You decide the exact formula :document it in a markdown cell. • Type advantage: Implement at least a simpliﬁed type-e`ectiveness system (e.g., Fire > Grass > Water > Fire). This data should also come from a database table. • Battle log: Display what happens each turn (who attacked, how much damage, super-e`ective or not, etc.). • Win condition: Game ends when all Pokémon on one side faint (HP ≤ 0). You may implement Player vs. Player or Player vs. AI (AI can use simple random moves). Task 3.3 :Cheat Codes (5pt) 
Implement a cheat code system that modiﬁes the database to give an unfair advantage. Implement at least 3 dicerent cheats, for example: • UPUPDOWNDOWN :doubles your Pokémon's HP via an UPDATE query • GODMODE :sets all your Pokémon's Defense and Sp.Def to 999 • STEAL :copies the opponent's strongest Pokémon to your team via INSERT • LEGENDARY :inserts a custom overpowered Pokémon into the database • NERF :reduces all opponent stats by 50% Key rule: Each cheat must work by executing a real SQL/ORM write operation (INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE) on the database :not just changing a Python variable in memory. After the game ends, demonstrate a "cheat audit" querythat can detect which cheats were used (e.g., Pokémon with stats exceeding the dataset's natural maximum). Task 3.4 :Pokémon Analysis (5pt) Write SQL/ORM queries to ﬁnd and report 2 interesting insights about the Pokémon dataset, for example: • Which type combination is the most overpowered on average? • Is there statistical evidence of "power creep" across generations? • What is the optimal 3-Pokémon team based purely on stats? • Which Legendary Pokémon is statistically the weakest?  Task 4: SQL Learning Game (25pt + bonus) 
⭐ The best and most creative solution will earn bonus points on the ﬁnal course grade. Goal: Build an interactive game or platform that teaches SQL to beginners. Your target audience is someone who has never written a SQL query before. Task 4.1 :Core Platform (15pt) Your platform must include: • A real SQLite database running in the background with at least one preloaded dataset that the learner queries against. 
• An interactive SQL input where the learner writes and executes real SQL queries. • At least 5 progressive challenges/levels that teach SQL concepts in order (e.g., Level 1: SELECT *, Level 2: WHERE, Level 3: ORDER BY / LIMIT, Level 4: GROUP BY / aggregations, Level 5: JOIN). • Feedback system: For each challenge, check whether the learner's query returns the correct result. If wrong, provide a helpful hint :not just "incorrect" , but an explanation of what went wrong or what to try. • Progress tracking: The learner can see which levels they have completed. Task 4.2 :Engagement & Creativity (10pt) This is where you di`erentiate yourself. Make your platform genuinely fun and memorable. Some ideas (or invent your own): • Story-driven: The learner is a detective solving crimes by querying an evidence database, or a wizard casting "SQL spells" , or a space explorer querying star charts • Gamiﬁcation: Points, streaks, leaderboard, timed challenges, achievements, badges • Visual: Show database tables visually, animate what JOIN or GROUP BY does, highlight which rows a WHERE clause selects • Adaptive diciculty: If the learner struggles, o`er simpler sub-challenges or step-by-step query building • Multiplayer/competitive: Two players race to solve SQL challenges, or one player writes a query and the other guesses what it returns • AI-powered hints: Use an LLM API to generate personalized hints based on the learner's incorrect query Submission for Task 4 • A short demo video (2–3 min) • A README explaining your design choices and the SQL concepts your platform teaches
